title: Pfeffernüsse
subtitle: German pepper-spice Christmas biscuits
excerpt: Small, dense, spiced biscuits that harden after baking to a chewy-crisp texture. Anise, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, and black pepper, the flavour is warm and complex and improves for days after baking. Rolled in icing sugar while still hot.
sourceNotes: Pfeffernüsse (pepper nuts) are documented in German Christmas baking from at least the eighteenth century. Early recipes appear in German household manuals of the 1800s and in the Weihnachtsgebäck (Christmas biscuit) canon of Alsace and northern Germany. The spice blend varies by region; this follows the Rhineland version that includes both black pepper and anise.

content.text: Pfeffernüsse come out of the oven looking unimpressive, small, pale, dense rounds. They're rolled in icing sugar while still hot, and then left alone for two or three days. The flavour deepens considerably on standing; the texture shifts from hard to a chewy-crisp that is very particular to this biscuit. Traditional recipes sometimes use
as the leavening; the modern baking-powder substitute produces a slightly different but acceptable result.

Beat the softened butter with the muscovado sugar and treacle until combined. Beat in the egg. Sift the flour, spices, baking powder, and bicarbonate of soda together, then add to the butter mixture and stir until a stiff dough forms. Wrap and refrigerate for 1 hour.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line baking trays with baking paper or silicone mats. Roll the dough into balls about 2 cm in diameter (roughly 10 to 12 g each) and place 3 cm apart on the trays. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until set and slightly cracked on the surface. They should look firm but not deeply coloured. Do not overbake; they harden further on cooling.

Working quickly while the biscuits are still hot, toss them in the icing sugar to coat evenly, then cool completely on a wire rack. Store in a tin for 2 to 3 days before eating, the flavour improves markedly with time.
